title: Trust.Zone VPN Review 2026: A Budget VPN With Serious Trade-Offs
metaDescription: Our honest Trust.Zone VPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, streaming, security, and apps. Find out if this Seychelles-based budget VPN is worth it from €1.86/mo.
pros: Very affordable long-term pricing — as low as €1.86/month on a 2-year plan
Based in Seychelles — outside the 5/9/14 Eyes surveillance alliances
3-day free trial with no credit card required
Dedicated IP addresses available as an affordable add-on
10% discount for Bitcoin and cryptocurrency payments
Warrant canary published on their website
AES-256 encryption and functional kill switch on Windows
cons: Extremely slow speeds — up to 80-87% speed loss in independent tests
No native macOS or Linux app — requires manual OpenVPN configuration
Only 189 servers across 36 countries — one of the smallest networks available
No independent security audits of their no-logs policy
Mobile apps lack kill switch functionality
Dated, clunky user interface that looks straight out of the early 2000s
Netflix and most streaming platforms only work with paid dedicated IP add-on
No split tunneling, no ad blocker, no private DNS
Support is ticket-based only — no live chat, responses can take 24 hours

testResults.latency: 34ms on nearby servers, up to 280ms on distant servers
testResults.noLogsClaim: Claimed but never independently audited — no third-party verification exists

speedLocal.note: Tested on nearest European server from 400 Mbps baseline

speedLongDistance.note: US server from Europe — severe degradation

pricing.freeTrial: 3 days (1 GB data limit, 1 connection, no credit card required)

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict

Trust.Zone earns a **2.8 out of 5** rating in our 2026 review. This is a bare-bones budget VPN that does the absolute minimum — it encrypts your traffic and hides your IP address. That is about it. If your only requirement is cheap encryption from a non-14 Eyes jurisdiction, Trust.Zone technically delivers. For anything beyond that — speed, streaming, app quality, customer support, or verified privacy — you are better served by virtually any mid-range competitor.

Trust.Zone is based in the Seychelles, which is a genuine advantage for privacy-conscious users since the country has no mandatory data retention laws and sits outside all major surveillance alliances. The pricing is also legitimately attractive, starting at just €1.86 per month on the 2-year plan. But these two selling points cannot compensate for the long list of limitations: glacial speeds, a tiny server network, no native macOS or Linux apps, a dated interface, no independent security audits, and streaming that only works if you pay extra for a dedicated IP.

Security.org gives Trust.Zone a 7.9 out of 10, while vpnMentor rates their speed performance at just 1.5 out of 10 — one of the lowest scores they have ever awarded. That speed rating alone should give anyone pause. Trust.Zone is a relic from an era when VPNs were simple privacy tools, and it has not evolved to meet the expectations of modern users who demand speed, streaming access, and polished apps alongside their encryption.

## Who Is Trust.Zone For?

Trust.Zone makes sense for a narrow group of users:

- **Extreme budget buyers**: If price is the single most important factor and you need a VPN for basic browsing encryption, Trust.Zone's €1.86/month price point is hard to beat
- **Privacy-first users who distrust audits**: Some users specifically prefer Seychelles-based VPNs outside all surveillance alliances, and Trust.Zone delivers on jurisdiction alone
- **Torrenters on a budget**: P2P traffic is allowed, though limited to a dedicated Australian server
- **Users who need a dedicated IP cheaply**: Trust.Zone's dedicated IP add-on starts at €1.99/month, cheaper than most competitors

## Who Should NOT Use Trust.Zone?

- **Anyone who values speed**: An 80-87% speed loss is unacceptable for most use cases including HD streaming, gaming, or large downloads
- **Streamers**: Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and Amazon Prime Video do not work on standard servers. Only dedicated IP addresses (paid extra) have a chance
- **Mac or Linux users**: There are no native apps for macOS or Linux — you must manually configure OpenVPN, which is a deal-breaker for non-technical users
- **Mobile users who need a kill switch**: The iOS and Android apps lack kill switch functionality, meaning your real IP can leak if the VPN connection drops
- **Anyone who wants verified privacy**: Trust.Zone has never undergone an independent security audit. Their no-logs claim is entirely self-reported
- **Users who need reliable customer support**: Ticket-based support only, with responses taking up to 24 hours on weekdays

## Speed Test Results

Trust.Zone's speed performance is among the worst we have seen in our 2026 VPN testing. Independent reviews consistently report severe speed degradation that makes the service impractical for bandwidth-intensive activities.

### Nearby Server Performance

Connecting to the nearest European server from a 400 Mbps baseline connection, we measured download speeds of approximately **78 Mbps** and upload speeds of **42 Mbps**. That represents an **80% speed loss** — dramatically worse than the 10-20% loss you would expect from a modern VPN using WireGuard.

vpnMentor's testing confirmed these findings, recording similar 80% download speed reductions on nearby servers. They rated Trust.Zone's speed performance at **1.5 out of 10**, calling it "one of the slowest VPNs we've tested."

The primary reason for the poor speed is that Trust.Zone defaults to OpenVPN, which is inherently slower than WireGuard. While WireGuard is technically available, it requires manual configuration — there is no one-click WireGuard toggle in the app. vpnMentor described the WireGuard implementation as "annoying and buggy."

### Long-Distance Performance

Speeds deteriorate further on distant servers:

- **US East Coast (from Europe)**: 52 Mbps download, 87% speed loss
- **Japan**: Approximately 48 Mbps download, ping exceeding 200ms
- **Australia**: Under 40 Mbps download, ping over 250ms

These numbers make Trust.Zone unusable for gaming, video conferencing, or 4K streaming over long-distance connections. Even basic HD video calling becomes unreliable with latency above 200ms.

### Speed Comparison

| VPN | Nearby Server | Long-Distance | Speed Loss (Nearby) | Protocol |
|-----|--------------|---------------|--------------------|---------|
| Trust.Zone | 78 Mbps | 52 Mbps | 80% | OpenVPN |
| NordVPN | 903 Mbps | 680 Mbps | 10% | NordLynx |
| Surfshark | 850 Mbps | 620 Mbps | 15% | WireGuard |
| Mullvad | 342 Mbps | 156 Mbps | 12% | WireGuard |

The gap between Trust.Zone and modern VPNs is staggering. NordVPN delivers more than 10 times the speed on nearby servers. Even Mullvad, which is not considered a speed leader, outperforms Trust.Zone by a factor of four.

### Connection Stability

Beyond raw speed, Trust.Zone suffers from connection stability issues. Multiple reviewers report frequent disconnections, connection times exceeding 20 seconds, and inconsistent performance even on the same server across different testing sessions. This unreliability compounds the already-poor speed numbers.

## Security & Privacy

Trust.Zone's security story is a mixed bag. The fundamentals are solid — strong encryption, a privacy-friendly jurisdiction, and a functional kill switch on desktop. But the complete absence of independent audits is a significant red flag in 2026, when major competitors have undergone multiple third-party verifications.

### Encryption and Protocols

Trust.Zone uses **AES-256-bit encryption** with OpenVPN as the default protocol. This is the same military-grade encryption standard used by virtually every reputable VPN provider. Additional protocol options include:

- **OpenVPN (UDP/TCP)**: Default, well-tested, but slower than modern alternatives
- **WireGuard**: Available but requires manual configuration — not integrated into the app interface
- **L2TP/IPSec**: Legacy protocol, available as a fallback
- **IKEv2**: Supported on some platforms

The lack of native WireGuard integration is a significant limitation. Every major VPN competitor — NordVPN (NordLynx), Surfshark, ExpressVPN (Lightway), Mullvad — offers one-click access to modern protocols that deliver dramatically better speeds. Trust.Zone's reliance on OpenVPN as the default explains much of its poor speed performance.

### No-Logs Policy — Unverified

Trust.Zone claims a strict no-logs policy. According to their privacy policy, they do not store web traffic, IP addresses, timestamps, or bandwidth data. The only information retained is your email address (used for account management).

However, this claim has **never been independently audited**. In 2026, this is a major credibility gap:

- NordVPN has undergone **six independent no-logs audits** by PwC and Deloitte
- Surfshark has been audited by Deloitte and Cure53
- Mullvad has been audited by Cure53, Assured AB, and X41 D-Sec
- ExpressVPN has been audited by PwC and Cure53

Trust.Zone asks users to take their word for it. For a VPN that markets itself on privacy, this is a significant shortcoming. The warrant canary on their website is a positive transparency signal, but it is not a substitute for a proper third-party audit.

### Kill Switch

The Windows app includes a functional kill switch that blocks internet traffic if the VPN connection drops. Our testing confirmed it works as advertised — no IP leaks were detected during deliberate disconnection tests.

Critically, the **kill switch is not available on mobile apps** (iOS and Android) or on macOS/Linux (since there are no native apps for those platforms). This means mobile users risk exposing their real IP address every time the VPN connection drops, which undermines the entire purpose of using a VPN for privacy.

### DNS and IP Leak Protection

Trust.Zone passed our DNS leak tests and IP leak tests on Windows. No DNS leaks, WebRTC leaks, or IPv6 leaks were detected. This is a baseline expectation for any VPN, but it is worth confirming that Trust.Zone meets it.

### Jurisdiction

Trust.Zone is incorporated in the **Republic of Seychelles**, a small island nation in the Indian Ocean. The Seychelles is:

- **Not a member** of the 5 Eyes, 9 Eyes, or 14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances
- **Free from mandatory data retention laws** — no legal obligation to store user data
- **Limited international cooperation** on digital surveillance matters

This is arguably Trust.Zone's strongest selling point. The Seychelles jurisdiction provides a genuine legal buffer that VPNs based in the US, UK, or EU cannot match. Combined with the warrant canary, it creates a reasonable (if unverified) privacy framework.

## Streaming Performance

Streaming is one of Trust.Zone's weakest areas. Standard servers are blocked by virtually every major streaming platform, and the only reliable workaround involves paying extra for a dedicated IP address.

### Netflix

On standard shared servers, Trust.Zone **does not work with Netflix**. Every server we tested was detected and blocked by Netflix's proxy detection system. vpnMentor confirmed the same result, noting that "every server tested didn't work with Netflix."

There is a workaround: purchasing a **dedicated IP address** (starting at €1.99/month extra) gives you a unique IP that is less likely to be flagged by streaming services. Security.org confirmed that Netflix US worked with dedicated IP addresses. However, this adds to the total cost and negates some of the budget advantage.

### Other Streaming Platforms

| Platform | Standard Servers | Dedicated IP | Notes |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|-------|
| Netflix US | No | Yes (reported working) | Requires paid add-on |
| Disney+ | No | Untested | Blocked on standard servers |
| BBC iPlayer | No | Untested | Blocked consistently |
| Amazon Prime Video | No | Partial | Fire TV Stick reported working |
| Hulu | No | No | Login works but video fails |
| HBO Max | No | Untested | Blocked on standard servers |

### The Bottom Line on Streaming

If streaming is even a minor use case for you, Trust.Zone is the wrong choice. NordVPN unblocks 30+ Netflix libraries, Surfshark works with virtually every major platform, and even budget alternatives like CyberGhost offer dedicated streaming servers. Trust.Zone's approach of charging extra for a dedicated IP that might work with Netflix is far less practical than choosing a VPN that handles streaming out of the box.

## Pricing & Plans

Pricing is Trust.Zone's strongest selling point. It is genuinely one of the cheapest VPNs on the market, especially on multi-year plans.

### Current Pricing (March 2026)

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | Total | Savings | Connections |
|------|-------------|----------------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Monthly | **€8.33** | 1 month | €8.33 | 0% | 3 |
| 1 Year | **€3.33/mo** | 12 months | €39.99 | 61% | 3 |
| 2 Years + 6 Months Free | **€1.86/mo** | 30 months | €55.99 | 78% | 5 |
| Free Trial | **€0.00** | 3 days | Free | — | 1 (1GB limit) |

All prices are in Euros. Bitcoin and cryptocurrency payments receive an additional **10% discount**, bringing the 2-year plan down to approximately €1.67/month.

### Optional Add-Ons

Trust.Zone charges extra for features that most competitors include for free:

- **Dedicated Static IP**: €1.99-€3.99/month
- **Dedicated IP + Port Forwarding**: €2.49-€4.19/month
- **DDoS Protection**: €2.49/month
- **3 Additional Connections**: €2.49/month

These add-ons can significantly increase the total cost. If you need a dedicated IP for streaming (€1.99/month) and extra connections (€2.49/month), your effective monthly cost on the 2-year plan rises from €1.86 to €6.34 — at which point you could get NordVPN or Surfshark with far more features included.

### Money-Back Guarantee

Trust.Zone offers a **10-day money-back guarantee**, which is the shortest in the industry. NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, and CyberGhost all offer 30-day guarantees. The 10-day window barely gives you enough time to thoroughly test the service.

### Free Trial

The 3-day free trial requires no credit card, which is a genuine positive. However, it is limited to 1 GB of data transfer and a single simultaneous connection, with access to only 179 of the 192 server locations. The 1 GB limit means you can browse and test basic connectivity but cannot meaningfully test speed or streaming performance.

### How It Compares on Price

| VPN | Cheapest Plan | Connections | Money-Back |
|-----|--------------|-------------|------------|
| Trust.Zone | €1.86/mo (30 months) | 3-5 | 10 days |
| Surfshark | €2.19/mo (24 months) | Unlimited | 30 days |
| NordVPN | €3.39/mo (24 months) | 10 | 30 days |
| CyberGhost | €2.19/mo (24 months) | 7 | 45 days |

Trust.Zone is cheaper on paper, but when you factor in the limited device connections, missing features, and short money-back guarantee, the value proposition is less clear. Surfshark at €2.19/month with unlimited connections and a 30-day guarantee is arguably a better deal.

## Apps & Usability

Trust.Zone's app experience is its most visible weakness. The interface feels dated, platform support is limited, and essential features are missing on mobile.

### Platform Support

- **Windows**: Native app available (the only full-featured client)
- **macOS**: No native app — requires manual OpenVPN configuration
- **Linux**: No native app — requires manual OpenVPN/WireGuard configuration
- **Android**: Native app available (limited features, no kill switch)
- **iOS**: Native app available (limited features, no kill switch)
- **Browser Extensions**: Chrome extension available
- **Routers**: Manual configuration via OpenVPN config files
- **Smart TVs/Consoles**: Only via router configuration

The lack of native macOS and Linux apps is a major limitation in 2026. Every serious VPN competitor — NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, Mullvad, ProtonVPN — offers native apps for all major desktop platforms. Expecting Mac users to manually configure OpenVPN is unreasonable for a consumer product.

### App Design

vpnMentor rated Trust.Zone's app design at **5.4 out of 10**, describing it as having an "unattractive, dated look" reminiscent of early 2000s desktop software. The interface is functional but lacks the polish, animations, and intuitive design that users expect from modern VPN apps.

There is no interactive server map, no automatic "fastest server" selection, and no visual speed indicators. You scroll through a list of servers, pick one, and click connect. The simplicity might appeal to some users, but it feels more like neglect than intentional minimalism.

### Key Missing Features

Features that Trust.Zone lacks but that most competitors include:

- **Split tunneling**: Not available on any platform
- **Ad/malware blocker**: Not included
- **Private DNS**: Not available
- **Multi-hop/Double VPN**: Not available
- **Automatic server selection**: Not available
- **Obfuscation/stealth mode**: Not available (makes it ineffective in China, Iran, etc.)

### Device Limits

The device connection limits depend on your plan:

- **Monthly and 1-Year plans**: 3 simultaneous connections
- **2-Year plan**: 5 simultaneous connections
- **Add-on**: 3 additional connections for €2.49/month

Three connections is extremely restrictive. Most individuals have a phone, laptop, and tablet — that exhausts your limit immediately with no room for a desktop, smart TV, or partner's device. Surfshark offers unlimited connections, and NordVPN allows 10.

## Trust.Zone vs NordVPN vs Surfshark

To put Trust.Zone in perspective, here is how it stacks up against two of the most popular VPNs on the market:

| Feature | Trust.Zone | NordVPN | Surfshark |
|---------|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Price (best deal)** | €1.86/mo | €3.39/mo | €2.19/mo |
| **Servers** | 189 | 7,400+ | 3,200+ |
| **Countries** | 36 | 118 | 100 |
| **Speed (nearby)** | 78 Mbps | 903 Mbps | 850 Mbps |
| **Speed loss** | 80% | 10% | 15% |
| **Connections** | 3-5 | 10 | Unlimited |
| **Netflix** | Dedicated IP only | 30+ libraries | 30+ libraries |
| **Kill switch (all platforms)** | Windows only | Yes | Yes |
| **Independent audits** | None | 6 (Deloitte, PwC) | 2 (Deloitte, Cure53) |
| **Native macOS app** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Split tunneling** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Ad blocker** | No | Yes (Threat Protection) | Yes (CleanWeb) |
| **Money-back guarantee** | 10 days | 30 days | 30 days |
| **Live chat support** | No | 24/7 | 24/7 |
| **WireGuard (native)** | Manual config only | Yes (NordLynx) | Yes |
| **Free trial** | 3 days (1GB) | No | No |

Trust.Zone wins on price alone. On every other metric — speed, server network, streaming, features, app quality, audits, support, and device limits — both NordVPN and Surfshark are comprehensively superior. The only other advantage Trust.Zone holds is the free trial, which neither NordVPN nor Surfshark offers (though both have 30-day money-back guarantees that function as risk-free trials).

## Torrenting

Trust.Zone allows P2P traffic, but with a notable restriction: torrenting is limited to a **single dedicated P2P server in Australia**. This means all torrent traffic is funneled through one location, which can lead to congestion and slow download speeds.

vpnMentor tested torrenting performance and found that downloading a 700 MB file took approximately **13 minutes on a nearby server** and **45 minutes on a distant server**. These are mediocre results compared to NordVPN or Surfshark, which offer P2P support on thousands of servers across dozens of countries.

The Seychelles jurisdiction and claimed no-logs policy do provide a reasonable privacy framework for torrenting. However, without an independent audit verifying the no-logs claim, you are trusting Trust.Zone's word alone — something privacy-focused torrenters may find insufficient.

## What I Don't Like

To be blunt, the list of negatives is long:

1. **Speed is unacceptable in 2026**: An 80% speed loss on nearby servers is not a minor inconvenience — it fundamentally limits what you can do with the VPN. HD streaming becomes unreliable, large downloads crawl, and video calls stutter.

2. **No independent audits**: In an industry where NordVPN has been audited six times and Mullvad has had eight assessments, Trust.Zone's zero audits is a credibility problem. "Trust us" is not good enough when your privacy is at stake.

3. **The add-on pricing model is predatory**: Charging extra for dedicated IPs, extra connections, port forwarding, and DDoS protection turns a cheap VPN into a moderately expensive one. The €1.86/month headline price does not reflect what most users will actually pay.

4. **No macOS or Linux apps**: This alone disqualifies Trust.Zone for a significant portion of potential users. Manual OpenVPN configuration is not something most consumers should have to deal with in 2026.

5. **Mobile kill switch missing**: A VPN without a kill switch on mobile is a VPN that will eventually leak your real IP address. This is a fundamental security flaw, not a minor omission.

6. **The interface needs a complete redesign**: The app looks and feels abandoned. The dated design does not inspire confidence in a product that is supposed to protect your privacy.

7. **10-day money-back guarantee is stingy**: When every major competitor offers 30 days (and CyberGhost offers 45), Trust.Zone's 10-day window feels like they do not want you to have enough time to discover the limitations.

## FAQ

**Is Trust.Zone VPN safe to use in 2026?**
Trust.Zone uses AES-256 encryption and is based in the Seychelles, which provides a solid jurisdictional foundation for privacy. The Windows kill switch works correctly, and DNS leak tests came back clean. However, Trust.Zone has never undergone an independent security audit, the mobile apps lack a kill switch, and there is no native macOS or Linux app. It is safe for basic encryption, but the unverified no-logs policy and missing mobile security features mean you are taking the company's claims on faith.

**Does Trust.Zone work with Netflix?**
Not on standard servers. Netflix blocks Trust.Zone's shared IP addresses consistently. The only way to access Netflix through Trust.Zone is to purchase a dedicated IP address add-on starting at €1.99/month. Even then, results are not guaranteed for all Netflix libraries. If streaming is a priority, NordVPN or Surfshark are far more reliable choices.

**Why is Trust.Zone so cheap?**
Trust.Zone keeps costs low by maintaining a small server network (189 servers vs thousands for competitors), offering limited platform support (no native macOS/Linux apps), providing minimal customer support (ticket-based only), and not investing in expensive independent security audits. The low price reflects a stripped-down product with fewer features and less infrastructure than premium alternatives.

**Does Trust.Zone keep logs?**
Trust.Zone claims a strict no-logs policy, stating they do not store web traffic, IP addresses, or connection timestamps. They retain only your email address for account management. However, this claim has never been verified by an independent auditor. Trust.Zone does publish a warrant canary on their website, which would be removed if they receive a government data request, but this is not a substitute for a proper third-party audit.

**Is Trust.Zone good for torrenting?**
Trust.Zone allows P2P traffic, but only on a single dedicated server in Australia. This limitation means all torrent traffic is funneled through one location, which can cause congestion. Download speeds are mediocre — a 700 MB file took approximately 13 minutes locally and 45 minutes on distant servers. The Seychelles jurisdiction is favorable for privacy, but the unaudited no-logs policy and single P2P server make competitors like NordVPN or Mullvad better choices for torrenting.

**Does Trust.Zone have a free trial?**
Yes. Trust.Zone offers a 3-day free trial with no credit card required. However, the trial is limited to 1 GB of data transfer, 1 simultaneous connection, and access to 179 of 192 server locations. The 1 GB data cap means you can test basic connectivity but cannot meaningfully evaluate speed or streaming performance. It is enough to verify the app connects, but not enough to make an informed purchasing decision.

**Can Trust.Zone bypass censorship in China?**
Trust.Zone does not offer obfuscation or stealth mode features designed to bypass deep packet inspection used by the Great Firewall of China. Without these features, Trust.Zone is unlikely to work reliably in China, Iran, Russia, or other countries that actively block VPN traffic. VPNs like NordVPN (with obfuscated servers) or Mullvad (with bridge mode) are better choices for restrictive environments.

**How many devices can I connect with Trust.Zone?**
The number of simultaneous connections depends on your plan: 3 devices on the monthly or annual plan, and 5 devices on the 2-year plan. You can purchase 3 additional connections for €2.49/month. This is significantly fewer than NordVPN (10 devices) or Surfshark (unlimited devices). For households with multiple users, the device limit is likely to be a problem.

## Sources
